ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for cheese foam tea specifically; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class. Taro and cheese foam components are not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code exists for cheese foam tea with taro in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to cheese foam taro tea as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one cup (400 g / 400 ml), as typically sold at Singaporean bubble tea and cheese tea outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Foam & Taro Yingde Black Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this specific beverage (cr_id G00010093). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from cream cheese and whipping cream composition using USDA FoodData Central reference values for cream cheese (FDC ID 173420) as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; estimated at approximately 13.0 g/100g based on fat fraction of cream cheese and whipping cream.

gi_source.title: Taro (Colocasia esculenta), cooked — Glycemic Index values; analogous estimate applied to taro-containing beverage
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese foam taro black tea as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on: (1) cooked taro GI of approximately 53 per University of Sydney GI Database for Colocasia esculenta; (2) black tea has negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect composite GI; (3) cream cheese foam contributes fat and protein which moderately suppress postprandial glucose response, further anchoring the composite GI in the low range. GI of 53 falls in the low category (≤55). Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (53 × 8.85 g carbohydrate per 100g × 4) / 100 = 18.8. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with direct measurement data when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Critically elevated sodium at 1279 mg/100g; a single 400 g serving delivers approximately 5116 mg sodium, exceeding twice the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension must avoid this beverage in its standard form; the sodium content from salted cream cheese foam alone far exceeds safe daily limits and will acutely raise blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.24 g sugar/100g (24.96 g per 400 g serving) and zero dietary fibre, producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; low-to-moderate GI of 53 combined with a glycemic load of ~18.8 per serving is clinically significant for a liquid beverage.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage; the liquid sugar from taro syrup and sweetened cheese foam is rapidly absorbed, and the absence of dietary fibre provides no glucose-buffering effect — request unsweetened taro paste, no added syrup, and unsweetened cheese foam if consuming.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of 13.0 g/100g from cream cheese and whipping cream, combined with sodium at 1279 mg/100g and cholesterol at 78 mg/100g, presents a triple cardiovascular risk burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage; the high saturated fat raises LDL cholesterol, the extreme sodium load elevates blood pressure, and the cholesterol content compounds atherogenic risk — plain Yingde black tea without cheese foam and syrup is a heart-healthy alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~13 g/100g promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine production; sodium at 1279 mg/100g exacerbates RA-related joint and tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; the high saturated fat and sodium synergistically worsen systemic inflammation and joint swelling — black tea polyphenols provide minor anti-inflammatory benefit but are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Foam and Taro Yingde Black Tea is a Singaporean-Chinese specialty beverage consisting of two distinct layers: a base of freshly brewed Yingde black tea blended with thick, hand-mashed taro paste, topped with a generous layer of salted cream cheese foam made from New Zealand cream cheese and whipping cream. The drink is designed to be consumed without stirring initially, so the drinker first experiences the savory, rich, and slightly salty cheese foam before it mingles with the sweet, starchy taro-infused black tea beneath. The taro paste contributes a dense, earthy sweetness and a smooth, velvety mouthfeel, while the Yingde black tea (from Guangdong province, China) provides a malty, slightly astringent base. Nutritionally, this beverage is calorie-dense at 307 kcal/100g, with a high fat content of 23.06 g/100g (predominantly saturated fat estimated at ~13 g/100g from dairy), high protein at 16.09 g/100g from cream cheese, and moderate carbohydrates at 8.85 g/100g. The sodium content is critically elevated at 1279 mg/100g due to the salted cheese foam, making a single 400 ml serving deliver over 5000 mg sodium. Calcium is exceptionally high at 1375 mg/100g. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its extreme sodium, saturated fat, and sugar load. It is popular as a breakfast or teatime drink in Singapore and is part of the broader Chinese cheese tea trend that emerged in mainland China around 2012 and spread to Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010093) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cream cheese reference data and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked taro (GI 53, low category).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (13.0 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central cream cheese entry (FDC ID 173420) and whipping cream composition, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this beverage. This value should be replaced with a direct laboratory measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this composite cheese foam taro tea beverage are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per the HPB source record; taro paste in this preparation is processed to a smooth paste and the tea base contributes negligible fibre. 4. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on cooked taro (Colocasia esculenta) GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; GI 53 falls in the low category (≤55). No direct GI measurement for this composite beverage exists in any approved source. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (53 × 8.85 × 4) / 100 = 18.8. 5. Halal status is marked false as the source does not confirm halal certification; the beverage may contain non-halal dairy processing aids.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.
Iteration 2: Verified gi_category alignment with glycemic_index value.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' by anchoring glycemic_index at 53 (cooked taro, University of Sydney GI Database), which falls in the low GI category (≤55). Updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 19.7 to 18.8 accordingly. Updated gi_source notes to reflect GI 53. Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reference GI 53. Updated confidence_reason and notes to reflect GI 53 low category.
